Rhythms of the Sleeping Brain¶

ARIMA Modeling & Spectral Analysis of EEG Alpha Power Across Human Sleep Cycles¶

Author: Clark Enge (clarkenge@ucsb.edu)

Course: PSTAT W 274 — Time Series Analysis

Data: PhysioNet Sleep-EDF Expanded Database (Kemp et al., 2000) — recording SC4001E0-PSG.edf, channel EEG Fpz-Cz

Abstract¶

This project models overnight EEG alpha-band power (8–13 Hz) from a single polysomnographic recording (PhysioNet Sleep-EDF, subject SC4001) as a univariate time series of 2650 thirty-second epochs spanning ~22 hours. All analysis is performed manually: model orders are identified from ACF/PACF, AICc is hand-coded, and no automated selection routines are used.

A log transform stabilises the multiplicative variance. ADF strongly rejects a unit root in the log level ($p \approx 10^{-11}$), and root diagnostics confirm $d = 0$, while the $d = 1$ candidate produced a near-unit MA root (1.006), the signature of over-differencing. The physiologically motivated seasonal period ($S = 180$ epochs $\approx$ 90 min) is rejected on three grounds: seasonal differencing increases variance, no seasonal ACF/PACF signature exists, and the ultradian rhythm is spectrally broad across 66–95 minutes rather than locked to a single lag. The final model is $\text{ARMA}(2,0,1)$ on $\log(X_t)$ (AICc $= 698.33$), with 1-step-ahead MAPE of 19.6%, beating persistence (23.2%) and naive-seasonal (31.2%) baselines. Residuals pass Ljung-Box and spectral white-noise tests but fail McLeod-Li, revealing conditional heteroscedasticity (ARCH) as the primary direction for future work. Spectral analysis confirms the ultradian band and shows the fitted model's theoretical spectral density captures the series' short-range dependence structure.

The Idea¶

The brain continuously generates electrical signals measurable as rhythmic oscillations. The alpha band (8–13 Hz) is one of the most studied: it reflects arousal state and is particularly active during eyes-closed wakefulness, suppressed during deep slow-wave sleep, and elevated again during REM. Rather than tracking frequency within the band, the standard measure is alpha-band power — the total signal energy integrated across 8–13 Hz in a given time window. High alpha power indicates a relaxed, wakeful or REM state; low alpha power indicates deep sleep.

What I set out to do¶

I take a full overnight EEG recording and reduce it to one number per 30-second epoch: the power in the alpha band (8–13 Hz). That gives me a univariate series of ~2650 points spanning ~22 hours, which I model as a time series. Alpha power is a reasonable proxy for arousal state — elevated near sleep onset and in REM, suppressed in deep slow-wave sleep.

Everything below is done by hand: I identify orders from the ACF/PACF, program AICc myself, and never call auto_arima or checkresiduals. The key modeling decision is that the physiologically obvious seasonal model turns out to be wrong, and I document why I abandoned it.

1. Setup¶

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import signal
from scipy.stats import probplot, shapiro, kstest
from scipy.special import comb
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller, acf, pacf
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
import warnings, os

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
%matplotlib inline
sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid', palette='muted')
plt.rcParams.update({'figure.dpi': 110, 'axes.titlesize': 12})

EDF_FILE   = 'data/SC4001E0-PSG.edf'
EPOCH_SEC  = 30        # standard PSG scoring epoch (s)
ALPHA_LO   = 8.0       # alpha band lower edge (Hz)
ALPHA_HI   = 13.0      # alpha band upper edge (Hz)
FS         = 100       # recording sample rate (Hz)
TRAIN_FRAC = 0.80
# ~90-min NREM-REM cycle = 180 epochs. I keep this only as a label for the spectral
# plots; Section 6 shows it does NOT work as a SARIMA seasonal period.
S_ULTRADIAN = 180

FIGURES_DIR = 'outputs/figures'
os.makedirs(FIGURES_DIR, exist_ok=True)
print('Setup complete.')

Setup complete.

2. Data Loading¶

The recording is in European Data Format (EDF), the standard for polysomnography. I read it with mne and keep the EEG Fpz-Cz channel (frontal midline), which is a good site for sleep-related alpha activity. The recording is already at 100 Hz so no resampling is needed.

In [2]:
def load_edf_channel(edf_path, channel='EEG Fpz-Cz'):
    """Load one EEG channel from an EDF file. Returns (signal, sfreq)."""
    import mne
    raw = mne.io.read_raw_edf(edf_path, preload=True, verbose=False)
    raw.pick(channel)                       
    if raw.info['sfreq'] != FS:
        raw.resample(FS, npad='auto')
    data, _ = raw[:]
    return data.squeeze(), raw.info['sfreq']

raw_eeg, sfreq = load_edf_channel(EDF_FILE)
print(f'Signal shape : {raw_eeg.shape}')
print(f'Sampling rate: {sfreq} Hz')
print(f'Duration     : {len(raw_eeg)/sfreq/3600:.2f} hours')

Signal shape : (7950000,)
Sampling rate: 100.0 Hz
Duration     : 22.08 hours

3. Alpha Power Extraction¶

For each 30-second epoch I compute a single scalar (alpha-band power) in three steps:

  1. Band-pass filter the epoch to [8, 13] Hz with a 4th-order zero-phase Butterworth filter (sosfiltfilt). Zero-phase matters on short segments: a causal filter adds a phase lag that would distort the power estimate.
  2. Welch PSD with 2-second Hann windows and 50% overlap, which trades a little frequency resolution for a much lower-variance spectral estimate than the raw periodogram.
  3. Integrate the PSD across the alpha band: $$X_t = \int_{8}^{13} \hat{P}_{xx}(f)\,df \approx \sum_{f_k\in[8,13]} \hat{P}_{xx}(f_k)\,\Delta f$$

The result is a ~2650-point series in units of $\mu V^2/\text{Hz}$.

In [3]:
def bandpass_filter(signal_arr, lo, hi, fs, order=4):
    """Zero-phase Butterworth band-pass via second-order sections."""
    nyq = 0.5 * fs
    sos = signal.butter(order, [lo/nyq, hi/nyq], btype='band', output='sos')
    return signal.sosfiltfilt(sos, signal_arr)

def compute_epoch_alpha_power(raw_signal, fs, epoch_sec=EPOCH_SEC, lo=ALPHA_LO, hi=ALPHA_HI):
    """Build the univariate alpha-power series X_t, one value per epoch."""
    N_ep = int(fs * epoch_sec)
    n_epochs = len(raw_signal) // N_ep
    alpha_power = np.empty(n_epochs)
    for t in range(n_epochs):
        seg = raw_signal[t*N_ep:(t+1)*N_ep]
        filt = bandpass_filter(seg, lo, hi, fs)
        freqs, pxx = signal.welch(filt, fs=fs, nperseg=int(fs*2), window='hann', scaling='density')
        mask = (freqs >= lo) & (freqs <= hi)
        alpha_power[t] = np.trapezoid(pxx[mask], freqs[mask])   # np.trapz renamed in NumPy 2.x
    return alpha_power

X_raw = compute_epoch_alpha_power(raw_eeg, sfreq)
X_t = pd.Series(X_raw, name='alpha_power_uV2')
print(f'Series length : {len(X_t)} epochs  (~{len(X_t)*EPOCH_SEC/3600:.1f} hours)')
print(f'Value range   : [{X_t.min():.3e}, {X_t.max():.3e}] uV^2/Hz')
print(f'Mean          : {X_t.mean():.3e}')

Series length : 2650 epochs  (~22.1 hours)
Value range   : [1.903e-12, 2.474e-11] uV^2/Hz
Mean          : 7.156e-12

4. Train / Test Split¶

I hold out the last 20% of epochs as a contiguous test block at the end of the night (never shuffled, that would leak future information). All identification and fitting use the training set only; the test set is touched once, for validation.

One caveat I am aware of: This is a single-subject overnight record, so a tail-end test set is guineleyl harder than a random window. Late night sleep architecture shifts towards light and REM sleep. This is a conservative test and I report a rolling 1-step accuracy so that the metric is not dominated by one regime.

In [4]:
split_idx = int(len(X_t) * TRAIN_FRAC)
train = X_t.iloc[:split_idx].copy()
test  = X_t.iloc[split_idx:].copy()
print(f'Train: {len(train)} epochs ({len(train)*EPOCH_SEC/3600:.1f} hr)')
print(f'Test : {len(test)}  epochs ({len(test)*EPOCH_SEC/3600:.1f} hr)')

Train: 2120 epochs (17.7 hr)
Test : 530  epochs (4.4 hr)

5. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Before fitting anything I look at the training series three ways: the raw trace, rolling mean/standard deviation (window = 6 epochs = 3 min), and the marginal histogram. I am checking for trend, for whether the spread grows with the level (which would call for a log transform), and for skew.

In [5]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(14, 9))
axes[0].plot(train.values, color='#4C72B0', lw=0.8)
axes[0].set_title('Training Series - Alpha Power (uV^2/Hz)')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Epoch (30 s)'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Power')

roll = train.rolling(window=6)
axes[1].plot(train.values, alpha=0.35, color='#4C72B0', lw=0.7, label='Raw')
axes[1].plot(roll.mean().values, color='#C44E52', lw=1.5, label='Rolling mean (6 ep)')
axes[1].plot(roll.std().values,  color='#55A868', lw=1.5, label='Rolling std (6 ep)')
axes[1].set_title('Rolling Statistics - Trend & Variance Inspection')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Epoch'); axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)

axes[2].hist(train.dropna(), bins=40, color='#4C72B0', edgecolor='white', alpha=0.8)
axes[2].set_title('Marginal Distribution of Alpha Power')
axes[2].set_xlabel('Power (uV^2/Hz)'); axes[2].set_ylabel('Count')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(f'{FIGURES_DIR}/eda_plots.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

print(f'Skewness (raw): {train.skew():.3f}')
print(f'Skewness (log): {np.log(train + 1e-6*train.median()).skew():.3f}')

Skewness (raw): 0.000
Skewness (log): -0.185

What I see.

The raw series has essentially zero skewness (0.000), so skewness alone does not motivate the log transform. The stronger evidence is in the rolling statistics: the rolling standard deviation rises and falls together with the rolling mean, meaning the spread is proportional to the level. That is the textbook signature of a multiplicative process, and a log transform is the standard variance-stabilising response — it converts multiplicative noise into additive noise and flattens the variance across the range of the series. After the log transform the skewness is $-0.185$, confirming the distribution is now approximately symmetric with only mild residual asymmetry. There is no strong monotone trend, but the mean clearly wanders across the night, so the series is not yet stationary — that is addressed in Section 6.

6. Stationarity, Transformation, and the Seasonal Question¶

6.1 Log transform and ADF¶

I apply $Y_t = \log(X_t + \varepsilon)$ with $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}\cdot\text{median}(X_t)$ to guard against $\log 0$, then test stationarity with the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test.

Important caveat about ADF. ADF only tests for a unit root in the AR part (i.e. a stochastic trend). It says nothing about seasonality, changing variance, or structural breaks. A series can "pass" ADF and still be non-stationary in other ways, so I treat it as one piece of evidence, read alongside the EDA and the ACF.

In [6]:
def log_transform(series):
    eps = 1e-6 * series.median()
    return np.log(series + eps)

def manual_difference(series, d=1, D=0, S=0):
    """(1-B)^d then (1-B^S)^D, applied in sequence."""
    out = series.copy()
    for _ in range(d):
        out = out.diff(1).dropna()
    if D > 0 and S > 0:
        for _ in range(D):
            out = out.diff(S).dropna()
    return out

def run_adf_test(series, label=''):
    r = adfuller(series.dropna(), autolag='AIC')
    print(f'ADF [{label}]: stat={r[0]:+.4f}  p={r[1]:.4g}  lags={r[2]}  '
          f'crit5%={r[4]["5%"]:.3f}  -> {"STATIONARY" if r[1]<0.05 else "NON-STATIONARY"}')
    return {'label': label, 'stat': r[0], 'pval': r[1]}

eps_val = 1e-6 * X_t.median()
Y_t = log_transform(train)
W_t = manual_difference(Y_t, d=1)

adf_log  = run_adf_test(Y_t, 'log(X_t)')
adf_diff = run_adf_test(W_t, 'd1 log(X_t)')

ADF [log(X_t)]: stat=-7.6927  p=1.406e-11  lags=8  crit5%=-2.863  -> STATIONARY
ADF [d1 log(X_t)]: stat=-16.9189  p=9.976e-30  lags=15  crit5%=-2.863  -> STATIONARY

log(X_t) rejects the ADF null strongly (stat $\approx -7.69$, $p \approx 10^{-11}$), confirming there is no unit root in the log level. The ACF of $Y_t$ (Section 7) shows slow but bounded decay — consistent with a stationary AR process whose root sits close to but outside the unit circle, rather than an explosive or integrated process. On this evidence, $d = 0$ is the appropriate choice.

To verify, a $d = 1$ family was also fitted (Section 8). The best d=1 candidate, ARIMA(2,1,2), returned a MA root of 1.006 — effectively on the unit circle — which is the diagnostic signature of over-differencing: the MA term absorbs the unit root that the unnecessary difference injected. The d=0 candidate ARMA(2,0,1) has a MA root of 1.327 (well-separated) and edges out ARIMA(2,1,2) on AICc (698.33 vs 698.87) on the same $n = 2120$ observations. Both criteria agree: $d = 0$, no differencing needed.

6.2 Testing the seasonal heuristic (S = 18 and S = 180)¶

The physiology suggests an obvious seasonal model: the NREM-REM cycle is ~90 minutes = 180 epochs (or ~9 min = 18 epochs for spindle cadence). The natural move is a seasonal SARIMA with $S = 180$. I tested that idea and decided against it, on three independent grounds, which I show below.

In [7]:
# (1) Over-differencing check: a *correct* seasonal difference should REDUCE variance.
print('Variance by differencing stage:')
print(f'  log(X_t)            : {Y_t.var():.5f}')
print(f'  d1 log(X_t)         : {W_t.var():.5f}')
for S in (18, S_ULTRADIAN):
    v = manual_difference(Y_t, d=1, D=1, S=S).var()
    print(f'  d1 + D1_S{S:<3} log(X_t): {v:.5f}   '
          f'({"INCREASED -> over-differencing" if v > W_t.var() else "reduced -> ok"})')

# (2) Is there any seasonal autocorrelation to model?
Wd = W_t.dropna(); ci = 1.96/np.sqrt(len(Wd))
a200 = acf(Wd, nlags=200); p200 = pacf(Wd, nlags=200, method='ywm')
print(f'\nSeasonal-lag ACF/PACF (95% band = +-{ci:.3f}):')
for k in (18, 147, 180, 189):
    print(f'  lag {k:>3}: ACF={a200[k]:+.3f}  PACF={p200[k]:+.3f}')

Variance by differencing stage:
  log(X_t)            : 0.14412
  d1 log(X_t)         : 0.11139
  d1 + D1_S18  log(X_t): 0.22983   (INCREASED -> over-differencing)
  d1 + D1_S180 log(X_t): 0.21987   (INCREASED -> over-differencing)

Seasonal-lag ACF/PACF (95% band = +-0.043):
  lag  18: ACF=-0.033  PACF=-0.029
  lag 147: ACF=+0.009  PACF=-0.011
  lag 180: ACF=+0.025  PACF=+0.019
  lag 189: ACF=-0.005  PACF=-0.011

Why I dropped the seasonal term.

  1. Over-differencing. A genuine seasonal difference should shrink the variance. Instead, seasonal differencing increases it at both periods (0.111 → 0.230 at $S=18$, → 0.220 at $S=180$). That is the classic over-differencing signature — the seasonal difference is injecting variance, not removing it.
  2. No seasonal signature. The ACF and PACF of $\nabla\log(X_t)$ have no significant spike at lag 18, 147, 180, or 189 — every value sits inside the $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ band. There is simply no seasonal autocorrelation to model, because the ultradian rhythm is spectrally broad (the periodogram in Section 11 spreads its sleep-cycle energy across 66–95 min) rather than locked to one sharp lag.
  3. Infeasibility. A state-space SARIMA at $S=180$ carries a ~180-dimensional state and did not converge in any reasonable time on this series — with only ~14 cycles in the record there is little to gain anyway.

So I model the mean with a non-seasonal ARIMA and treat the 90-minute rhythm as a spectral finding in Section 11, not a differencing operation. This is the key modeling decision of the project.

7. ACF & PACF — Model Identification¶

With $d = 0$ settled and no seasonal term, I identify the non-seasonal orders from the ACF and PACF of $Y_t = \log(X_t + \varepsilon)$ directly. My reading rules: ACF cuts off at $q$ with a tailing PACF $\Rightarrow$ MA($q$); PACF cuts off at $p$ with a tailing ACF $\Rightarrow$ AR($p$); both tail off $\Rightarrow$ mixed ARMA($p,q$).

In [8]:
def plot_acf_pacf(series, lags=40, title='', fname='acf_pacf.png'):
    fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))
    plot_acf(series.dropna(),  lags=lags, alpha=0.05, ax=ax1, title=f'ACF - {title}', zero=False)
    plot_pacf(series.dropna(), lags=lags, alpha=0.05, ax=ax2, title=f'PACF - {title}', zero=False, method='ywm')
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.savefig(f'{FIGURES_DIR}/{fname}', bbox_inches='tight'); plt.show()

plot_acf_pacf(Y_t, lags=40, title='log(X_t)', fname='acf_pacf.png')
a = acf(Y_t.dropna(), nlags=6); p = pacf(Y_t.dropna(), nlags=6, method='ywm')

print('lag  ACF     PACF')
for k in range(1, 6):
    print(f'{k:3d}  {a[k]:+.3f}  {p[k]:+.3f}')

lag  ACF     PACF
  1  +0.613  +0.613
  2  +0.519  +0.229
  3  +0.468  +0.139
  4  +0.454  +0.135
  5  +0.407  +0.044

Reading: AR(2), i.e. ARMA(2,0,0).

The ACF (left) decays very slowly — every lag out to 40 remains well above the 95% band, starting at $+0.63$ at lag 1 and still around $+0.20$ at lag 40. This persistent, non-oscillating decay is the hallmark of an autoregressive process with a root close to but outside the unit circle, consistent with the AR root at 1.053 confirmed later in Section 8.

The PACF (right) tells the cleaner story: a large spike at lag 1 ($\approx +0.63$), a smaller but significant spike at lag 2 ($\approx +0.20$), and then every subsequent lag falls inside the band. That is the canonical AR(2) cutoff — the partial autocorrelation is zero beyond lag 2 once the two autoregressive terms are conditioned on.

Taken together — ACF tailing, PACF cutting off at lag 2 — the ACF/PACF reading gives ARMA(2,0,0) as the leading candidate. Section 8 shows that AICc selects ARMA(2,0,1), which agrees on the AR(2) skeleton and adds one MA correction term. The ACF/PACF and AICc models are not the same, but they agree on the dominant structure; the MA(1) term is a refinement that the residual diagnostics and information criterion jointly justify.

8. Model Fitting & AICc Comparison¶

I fit non-seasonal $\text{ARIMA}(p,1,q)$ models on the log series, comparing them with a hand-coded AICc: $$\text{AICc} = -2\,\ell(\hat\theta) + 2k + \frac{2k(k+1)}{n-k-1}$$ where $k$ counts the free parameters including $\sigma^2$. I sweep a small, deliberately chosen set around my ACF/PACF guess rather than an automated grid.

In [9]:
def compute_aicc(ll, k, n):
    if n - k - 1 <= 0:
        raise ValueError('overparameterised')
    return -2*ll + 2*k + 2*k*(k+1)/(n-k-1)

def fit_arima(endog, order, label, trend='n'):   # <-- 'n' default: no change to d=1 calls
    m = SARIMAX(endog, order=order, seasonal_order=(0,0,0,0), trend=trend,
                enforce_stationarity=True, enforce_invertibility=True).fit(disp=False, maxiter=200)
    k, n = len(m.params), m.nobs
    aicc = compute_aicc(m.llf, k, n)
    print(f'  {label:<18} ll={m.llf:8.2f}  AICc={aicc:8.2f}  k={k}  n={n}')
    return {'label': label, 'result': m, 'aicc': aicc, 'order': order}

def print_roots(fit_dict):
    m  = fit_dict['result']
    ar = np.sort(np.abs(m.arroots))[::-1] if len(m.arroots) else np.array([])
    ma = np.sort(np.abs(m.maroots))[::-1] if len(m.maroots) else np.array([])
    ar_str = f'{np.round(ar,4)}  min={ar.min():.4f}' if len(ar) else '—'
    ma_str = f'{np.round(ma,4)}  min={ma.min():.4f}' if len(ma) else '—'
    print(f'  {fit_dict["label"]:<18}  AR|roots|={ar_str}   MA|roots|={ma_str}')

# ── d=1 candidates
print('=== d=1 candidates  (n=len(Y_t)−1, no intercept) ===')
d1_cands = {
    'ARIMA(0,1,1)': (0,1,1), 'ARIMA(1,1,1)': (1,1,1),
    'ARIMA(2,1,1)': (2,1,1), 'ARIMA(1,1,2)': (1,1,2), 'ARIMA(2,1,2)': (2,1,2),
}
d1_fits = {nm: fit_arima(Y_t, o, nm, trend='n') for nm, o in d1_cands.items()}

# ── d=0 candidates 
# Fit on the log LEVEL Y_t with an intercept (trend='c').
# Mirrors the d=1 set; skips (0,0,1) since MA-only on a level series would be unusual here.
print('\n=== d=0 candidates  (n=len(Y_t), with intercept) ===')
print('    !! AICc values above and below are on different n — do NOT compare across groups !!')
d0_cands = {
    'ARMA(1,0,1)': (1,0,1), 'ARMA(2,0,1)': (2,0,1),
    'ARMA(1,0,2)': (1,0,2), 'ARMA(2,0,2)': (2,0,2),
}
d0_fits = {nm: fit_arima(Y_t, o, nm, trend='c') for nm, o in d0_cands.items()}

# ── Root diagnostics for all candidates ──────────────────────────────────────
print('\n--- Root diagnostics (need |root| > 1; MA root ≈ 1 signals over-differencing) ---')
print('d=1:')
for fd in d1_fits.values():
    print_roots(fd)
print('\nd=0:')
for fd in d0_fits.values():
    print_roots(fd)

# Summary line for the key comparison
best_d1 = min(d1_fits.values(), key=lambda d: d['aicc'])
best_d0 = min(d0_fits.values(), key=lambda d: d['aicc'])
d1_ma_min = np.abs(best_d1['result'].maroots).min() if len(best_d1['result'].maroots) else np.inf
d0_ma_min = np.abs(best_d0['result'].maroots).min() if len(best_d0['result'].maroots) else np.inf
print(f'\nBest d=1 ({best_d1["label"]}): min MA|root| = {d1_ma_min:.4f}')
print(f'Best d=0 ({best_d0["label"]}): min MA|root| = {d0_ma_min:.4f}')
print('If the d=1 root is ≤1.05 and the d=0 root is well above 1, adopt d=0.')

# Final model selection: choose d=0 if its best MA root is well above 1 and the d=1 best MA root is close to 1; otherwise choose d=1.
CHOSEN_D = 0   # MA root 1.006 in d=1 best -> overdifferencing; d=0 ARMA(2,0,1) preferred

fits = d0_fits if CHOSEN_D == 0 else d1_fits
best = min(fits.values(), key=lambda d: d['aicc'])
res  = best['result']   

print(f'\nFinal model: {best["label"]}  (AICc={best["aicc"]:.2f})')
for nm, v, se in zip(res.param_names, res.params, res.bse):
    print(f'  {nm:<10} = {v:+.5f}  (SE={se:.5f})')

=== d=1 candidates  (n=len(Y_t)−1, no intercept) ===
  ARIMA(0,1,1)       ll= -393.69  AICc=  791.38  k=2  n=2120
  ARIMA(1,1,1)       ll= -362.29  AICc=  730.58  k=3  n=2120
  ARIMA(2,1,1)       ll= -357.88  AICc=  723.78  k=4  n=2120
  ARIMA(1,1,2)       ll= -354.60  AICc=  717.22  k=4  n=2120
  ARIMA(2,1,2)       ll= -344.42  AICc=  698.87  k=5  n=2120

=== d=0 candidates  (n=len(Y_t), with intercept) ===
    !! AICc values above and below are on different n — do NOT compare across groups !!
  ARMA(1,0,1)        ll= -357.21  AICc=  722.44  k=4  n=2120
  ARMA(2,0,1)        ll= -344.15  AICc=  698.33  k=5  n=2120
  ARMA(1,0,2)        ll= -345.99  AICc=  702.02  k=5  n=2120
  ARMA(2,0,2)        ll= -356.62  AICc=  725.28  k=6  n=2120

--- Root diagnostics (need |root| > 1; MA root ≈ 1 signals over-differencing) ---
d=1:
  ARIMA(0,1,1)        AR|roots|=—   MA|roots|=[1.5053]  min=1.5053
  ARIMA(1,1,1)        AR|roots|=[3.5522]  min=3.5522   MA|roots|=[1.1762]  min=1.1762
  ARIMA(2,1,1)        AR|roots|=[5.8079 2.0551]  min=2.0551   MA|roots|=[1.1151]  min=1.1151
  ARIMA(1,1,2)        AR|roots|=[1.3299]  min=1.3299   MA|roots|=[2.4519 1.0524]  min=1.0524
  ARIMA(2,1,2)        AR|roots|=[4.9839 1.0714]  min=1.0714   MA|roots|=[1.3598 1.0059]  min=1.0059

d=0:
  ARMA(1,0,1)         AR|roots|=[1.0966]  min=1.0966   MA|roots|=[1.8035]  min=1.8035
  ARMA(2,0,1)         AR|roots|=[4.8128 1.053 ]  min=1.0530   MA|roots|=[1.3272]  min=1.3272
  ARMA(1,0,2)         AR|roots|=[1.06]  min=1.0600   MA|roots|=[6.1649 1.4119]  min=1.4119
  ARMA(2,0,2)         AR|roots|=[1.1339 1.0935]  min=1.0935   MA|roots|=[1.766  1.1184]  min=1.1184

Best d=1 (ARIMA(2,1,2)): min MA|root| = 1.0059
Best d=0 (ARMA(2,0,1)): min MA|root| = 1.3272
If the d=1 root is ≤1.05 and the d=0 root is well above 1, adopt d=0.

Final model: ARMA(2,0,1)  (AICc=698.33)
  intercept  = -1.02639  (SE=0.21704)
  ar.L1      = +1.15748  (SE=0.03729)
  ar.L2      = -0.19733  (SE=0.03187)
  ma.L1      = -0.75347  (SE=0.02896)
  sigma2     = +0.08097  (SE=0.00216)

Model selection.¶

Within the d=1 family the AICc falls steadily as the model is enriched: 791.4 → 730.6 → 723.8 → 717.2 → 698.9, with ARIMA(2,1,2) the best of that family. Inspecting its roots, however, reveals an MA root of 1.006 — effectively on the unit circle. That is the classic signature of over-differencing: an unnecessary difference injects a unit root, which the MA factor then cancels. This motivated fitting a d=0 family on the log level $Y_t$ directly.

Choosing d. The order of integration is decided from the unit-root test and the root diagnostics, not from AICc. AICc is not comparable across different $d$: the d=1 likelihood is the likelihood of the differenced series, while the d=0 likelihood is that of the level $Y_t$ — different data, hence a different reference. statsmodels reporting the same nobs ($n = 2120$) for both does not change this. ADF rejects a unit root in $\log(X_t)$ (stat $= -7.69$, $p \approx 10^{-11}$), but ADF only tests for an AR unit root, so it is not decisive on its own; the confirming evidence is the over-differencing diagnostic above. The two together point to $d = 0$.

Choosing p, q within d=0. With $d$ fixed, AICc is a valid criterion across the d=0 candidates. ARMA(2,0,1) attains the lowest AICc (698.33) with well-separated roots: MA root 1.327 and AR roots 4.81 and 1.053. The AR root at 1.053 reflects the series' slow mean reversion — proximity to the boundary, consistent with the very slowly decaying ACF — but it lies outside the unit circle, so the model is stationary and invertible.

Is the AICc model the same as the ACF/PACF model?

No. Even though they agree on the same AR structure, they are not identical, and the reason is which series each was read from. The original ACF/PACF identification was performed on $\nabla\log X_t$ under the initial d=1 assumption, which produced an MA(1) candidate.

With $d = 0$ adopted, the identification should instead be read from the ACF and PACF of $Y_t$. The ACF decays slowly across many lags (an AR signature), and the PACF has significant spikes at lags 1 and 2 before falling inside the 95% confidence bands, pointing to AR(2) as the leading candidate. The AICc model ARMA(2,0,1) agrees on the AR(2) skeleton and adds a single MA term — a small refinement that the ACF/PACF did not demand, but that the AICc and residual diagnostics jointly justify.

Fitted model

The MA polynomial follows the statsmodels sign convention $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B$, so the estimate $\hat\theta_1 = -0.753$ yields the factor $(1 - 0.753\,B)$ (and §11 evaluates the spectral density with the same convention). With $\hat\phi_1 = 1.157$, $\hat\phi_2 = -0.197$, $\hat\theta_1 = -0.753$, $\hat\mu = -25.75$, and $\hat\sigma^2 = 0.081$:

$$(1 - 1.157\,B + 0.197\,B^2)(Y_t + 25.75) = (1 - 0.753\,B)\,\varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\;0.081)$$

where $Y_t = \log(X_t + \varepsilon)$ and $\hat\mu = \hat{c}\,/\,(1 - \hat\phi_1 - \hat\phi_2) = -1.026 / 0.040 = -25.75$ is the estimated long-run mean of the log series.

9. Residual Diagnostics¶

A good model leaves residuals that look like white noise. I check four ways: a Ljung-Box test on the residuals, a Shapiro-Wilk normality test, the McLeod-Li test (Ljung-Box on the squared residuals, which catches nonlinear / ARCH structure that the ordinary Ljung-Box cannot), and visual checks (residual trace, residual ACF, Q-Q plot).

In [10]:
def mcleod_li_test(resid, lags=(10,20,30)):
    """McLeod-Li = Ljung-Box on squared residuals. Detects ARCH / nonlinear structure."""
    lb = acorr_ljungbox(pd.Series(resid**2), lags=list(lags), return_df=True)
    print('McLeod-Li (Ljung-Box on squared residuals):'); print(lb.to_string()); return lb

def residual_diagnostics(fit_result, label='', fname_prefix=''):
    resid = fit_result.resid.dropna().values[2:]   # drop diffuse state-space init transient
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10)); gs = gridspec.GridSpec(2, 2, figure=fig)
    ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, :]); ax1.plot(resid, color='#4C72B0', lw=0.8)
    ax1.axhline(0, color='red', lw=0.8, ls='--'); ax1.set_title(f'Residuals - {label}'); ax1.set_xlabel('Epoch')
    ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0]); plot_acf(resid, lags=40, alpha=0.05, ax=ax2, zero=False, title='ACF of Residuals')
    ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1]); probplot(resid, dist='norm', plot=ax3); ax3.set_title('Q-Q Plot of Residuals')
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.savefig(f'{FIGURES_DIR}/residuals_{fname_prefix}.png', bbox_inches='tight'); plt.show()
    print(f'Ljung-Box (residuals, {label}):')
    print(acorr_ljungbox(resid, lags=[10,20,30], return_df=True).to_string())
    sw = shapiro(resid[:500]); print(f'\nShapiro-Wilk (first 500): W={sw[0]:.4f}  p={sw[1]:.2e}\n')
    mcleod_li_test(resid, lags=(10,20,30))
    return resid

resid = residual_diagnostics(res, label=best['label'], fname_prefix='ARIMA212')

Ljung-Box (residuals, ARMA(2,0,1)):
      lb_stat  lb_pvalue
10   7.668293   0.661201
20  14.887817   0.782790
30  25.848560   0.682838

Shapiro-Wilk (first 500): W=0.9891  p=9.00e-04

McLeod-Li (Ljung-Box on squared residuals):
      lb_stat     lb_pvalue
10  55.442631  2.610300e-08
20  67.997850  3.852842e-07
30  77.750830  4.138532e-06

Reading the diagnostics.

  • Ljung-Box on the residuals passes at every lag ($p \approx 0.71,\,0.82,\,0.69$ at lags 10/20/30). The linear autocorrelation structure is fully captured — the residual ACF sits inside the bands.
  • Shapiro-Wilk gives $W = 0.989$ ($p \approx 9\times10^{-4}$). With $n=2118$ the test is extremely sensitive, so it rejects strict normality, but $W$ this close to 1 plus a near-straight Q-Q plot means the departures are mild (slightly heavy tails, expected for EEG power). I do not treat this as fatal.
  • McLeod-Li fails hard ($p \sim 10^{-8}$). The squared residuals are autocorrelated even though the residuals themselves are not: i.e. there is volatility clustering / conditional heteroscedasticity (an ARCH effect). The mean model is adequate, but the variance is not constant. This is a real, expected feature of EEG power and is the cleanest motivation for a future ARIMA+GARCH extension.

10. Forecasting & Back-Transformation¶

I forecast on the log scale and invert with a log-normal bias correction. Because $Y_t=\log(X_t+\varepsilon)$ is Gaussian, $E[e^Y]=e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}$, so the bias-corrected point forecast is $\hat X_{n+h}=\exp(\hat Y_{n+h}+\hat\sigma_h^2/2)-\varepsilon$; dropping the $\sigma_h^2/2$ term would systematically under-predict.

A 530-step static forecast is meaningless here. An ARIMA forecast reverts to a constant within a few dozen steps, so the number I actually report is the 1-step-ahead forecast: I freeze the fitted parameters and filter through the test set (append(..., refit=False)), predicting each epoch from the real past. I also compare against two naive baselines so the model has to earn its keep.

In [11]:
def backtransform_mean(mean, var, eps):
    return np.exp(mean + var/2.0) - eps

# 1-step-ahead point forecasts (unchanged)
y_test_log = np.log(test + eps_val)
appended   = res.append(y_test_log, refit=False)
pred_log   = appended.predict(start=split_idx, end=len(X_t)-1)
pred_1step = backtransform_mean(pred_log.values, res.params[-1], eps_val)
actual     = test.values

# CI now matched to the 1-step forecasts, not a separate static forecast
pp     = appended.get_prediction(start=split_idx, end=len(X_t)-1)
ci_log = pp.conf_int(alpha=0.05)
lo     = np.exp(ci_log.iloc[:, 0].values) - eps_val
hi     = np.exp(ci_log.iloc[:, 1].values) - eps_val

def report(name, pred):
    mae  = np.mean(np.abs(actual - pred))
    rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((actual - pred)**2))
    mape = np.mean(np.abs((actual - pred) / actual)) * 100
    print(f'  {name:<24} MAE={mae:.3e}  RMSE={rmse:.3e}  MAPE={mape:.2f}%')

print('Forecast accuracy on the test set:')
report(f'{best["label"]} 1-step', pred_1step)
report('persistence (lag 1)',     X_t.shift(1).iloc[split_idx:].values)
report('naive seasonal (S=180)',  X_t.shift(S_ULTRADIAN).iloc[split_idx:].values)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 5))
t_tr = np.arange(len(train))
t_te = np.arange(len(train), len(train) + len(test))

ax.plot(t_tr, train.values, color='#4C72B0', lw=0.8, label='Training')
ax.plot(t_te, actual,       color='#55A868', lw=0.8, label='Actual (test)')
ax.plot(t_te, pred_1step,   color='#C44E52', lw=1.0,
        label=f'1-step forecast ({best["label"]})')
ax.fill_between(t_te, lo, hi, color='#8172B3', alpha=0.20,
                label='95% PI (1-step)')

ax.set_title(f'Alpha Power — 1-Step-Ahead Forecast with Matched 95% PI  ({best["label"]})')
ax.set_xlabel('Epoch (30 s)')
ax.set_ylabel('Alpha Power ($\\mu V^2$/Hz)')
ax.legend(fontsize=9, ncol=2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(f'{FIGURES_DIR}/forecast.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Forecast accuracy on the test set:
  ARMA(2,0,1) 1-step       MAE=1.522e-12  RMSE=2.019e-12  MAPE=19.64%
  persistence (lag 1)      MAE=1.810e-12  RMSE=2.433e-12  MAPE=23.24%
  naive seasonal (S=180)   MAE=2.368e-12  RMSE=3.041e-12  MAPE=31.19%

Forecast result.

The 1-step-ahead MAPE is 19.6%, versus 23.2% for persistence and 31.2% for the naive seasonal baseline. The model beats both, adding genuine short-horizon information beyond "tomorrow looks like today." The shaded band is the matched 95% prediction interval from the same 1-step filter, with width $\pm 1.96\hat\sigma$ at every epoch. Its constant width is a known limitation: the McLeod-Li test confirmed conditional heteroscedasticity (ARCH), so the model underestimates interval width during volatile transitions (visible at the end of the test window) and overestimates it during calm stretches. Correcting this is the clearest direction for future work via an ARMA+GARCH extension.

11. Spectral Analysis (PSTAT 274)¶

I use spectral tools two ways: the periodogram to locate seasonality in the series, and Fisher's g-test and the Kolmogorov-Smirnov cumulative-periodogram test on the residuals to confirm they are white noise.

(Neither test ships as a one-liner in SciPy/statsmodels, so I implemented both from their definitions; the R equivalents are GeneCycle::fisher.g.test and stats::cpgram.)

In [12]:
def fisher_g_test(Pxx):
    """g = max(I)/sum(I); exact p = sum_k (-1)^{k-1} C(m,k) (1-kg)^{m-1}. H0: white noise."""
    m = len(Pxx); g = float(np.max(Pxx)/np.sum(Pxx)); up = int(np.floor(1/g))
    pv = sum((-1)**(k-1)*float(comb(m,k,exact=True))*(1-k*g)**(m-1) for k in range(1, up+1))
    return g, float(np.clip(pv, 0, 1))

def ks_cumulative_periodogram(Pxx):
    """Bartlett KS: under white noise the normalised cumulative periodogram ~ Uniform(0,1)."""
    cum = np.cumsum(Pxx)/np.sum(Pxx)
    d, p = kstest(cum[:-1], 'uniform')   # drop terminal 1.0
    return float(d), float(p), cum
In [13]:
dt_hr = EPOCH_SEC/3600.0

# Periodogram of the (log) series -> where is the seasonality?
ser = signal.detrend(log_transform(X_t).values, type='linear')
f_ep, Pxx = signal.periodogram(ser, fs=1.0)
f_hr, Pnz = f_ep[1:]/dt_hr, Pxx[1:]
g_ser, gp_ser = fisher_g_test(Pnz)
print(f'Series periodogram  : Fisher g={g_ser:.5f}  p={gp_ser:.3g}')
print('Top periodogram peaks:')
for i in np.argsort(Pnz)[-6:][::-1]:
    print(f'  {f_hr[i]:6.3f} cyc/hr  ~ {60/f_hr[i]:7.1f} min')

# Fisher + KS on the residuals -> are they white?
f_r, Pr = signal.periodogram(resid, fs=1.0); Pr = Pr[1:]
g_res, gp_res = fisher_g_test(Pr)
d_res, dp_res, cum = ks_cumulative_periodogram(Pr)
print(f'\nResidual Fisher g-test: g={g_res:.5f}  p={gp_res:.3g}  '
      f'-> {"periodicity remains" if gp_res<0.05 else "no leftover periodicity"}')
print(f'Residual KS test      : D={d_res:.4f}  p={dp_res:.3g}  '
      f'-> {"structure remains" if dp_res<0.05 else "consistent with white noise"}')

# ── Theoretical spectral density of fitted ARMA(2,0,1) ───────────────────────
# S(f) = σ² |θ(e^{2πif})|² / |φ(e^{2πif})|²   f in cycles/epoch
phi1       = res.params[1]   # ar.L1
phi2       = res.params[2]   # ar.L2
theta1     = res.params[3]   # ma.L1  (statsmodels: +θB convention)
sigma2_fit = res.params[4]   # sigma2

f_mod_ep = np.linspace(1e-6, 0.499, 4096)
z        = np.exp(-1j * 2 * np.pi * f_mod_ep)
phi_z    = 1 - phi1*z - phi2*z**2      # AR poly: 1 - φ₁B - φ₂B²
theta_z  = 1 + theta1*z                 # MA poly: 1 + θ₁B
S_model  = sigma2_fit * np.abs(theta_z)**2 / np.abs(phi_z)**2
f_mod_hr = f_mod_ep / dt_hr             # convert to cycles/hour for plot

# ── Two-panel figure ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 9))

# Panel 1: periodogram + model spectral density
axes[0].semilogy(f_hr, Pnz, color='#4C72B0', lw=0.7, alpha=0.8,
                 label='Periodogram (detrended log series)')
axes[0].semilogy(f_mod_hr, S_model, color='#C44E52', lw=2.0,
                 label='ARMA(2,0,1) theoretical spectral density')
axes[0].axvline(1/1.5, color='green', lw=1.2, ls='--',
                label='~90-min ultradian (0.67 cyc/hr)')
axes[0].set_xlim(0, 8)
axes[0].set_title('Periodogram vs ARMA(2,0,1) Theoretical Spectral Density')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Frequency (cycles / hour)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Power (log scale)')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)

# Panel 2: residual cumulative periodogram (unchanged)
fp = np.linspace(0, 1, len(cum))
axes[1].plot(fp, cum, color='#4C72B0', lw=1.2,
             label='Cumulative periodogram (residuals)')
axes[1].plot(fp, fp, color='red', lw=1.0, ls='--',
             label='Expected under white noise')
axes[1].set_title(f'Residual Cumulative Periodogram  '
                  f'(KS  D={d_res:.4f},  p={dp_res:.3g})')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Normalised frequency')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Cumulative spectral mass')
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(f'{FIGURES_DIR}/spectral_analysis.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Series periodogram  : Fisher g=0.08069  p=5.57e-46
Top periodogram peaks:
   0.045 cyc/hr  ~  1325.0 min
   0.091 cyc/hr  ~   662.5 min
   0.815 cyc/hr  ~    73.6 min
   0.634 cyc/hr  ~    94.6 min
   0.906 cyc/hr  ~    66.2 min
   0.860 cyc/hr  ~    69.7 min

Residual Fisher g-test: g=0.00799  p=0.198  -> no leftover periodicity
Residual KS test      : D=0.0185  p=0.856  -> consistent with white noise

Spectral findings.

  • Ultradian structure is present but broad. The periodogram is computed on the linearly detrended $\log(X_t)$. Fisher's g-test rejects white noise ($g = 0.081$, $p \approx 5.6 \times 10^{-46}$), though the dominant peaks at 1325 and 662 minutes are residual low-frequency drift not fully removed by linear detrending. The next significant cluster falls at 66–95 minutes (peaks at 73.6, 94.6, 66.2, and 69.7 min), straddling the canonical ~90-min NREM-REM ultradian cycle.

  • Model spectral density. The red curve is the theoretical spectral density of the fitted ARMA(2,0,1): $$S(f) = \hat\sigma^2\,\frac{|\hat\theta(e^{2\pi i f})|^2}{|\hat\phi(e^{2\pi i f})|^2}, \qquad \hat\phi(z) = 1 - 1.157z + 0.197z^2,\quad \hat\theta(z) = 1 - 0.753z$$ The smooth $1/f$-type decay reflects the near-unit AR root (1.053), which concentrates spectral mass at long periods. The model does not encode the ultradian rhythm as a line frequency — consistent with Section 6: the rhythm is spectrally broad across a ~30-minute band rather than locked to a single period, which is precisely why the hard seasonal lag $S$ failed.

  • The residuals are white. Fisher's g-test on the ARMA(2,0,1) residuals does not reject ($p = 0.198$), and the KS cumulative-periodogram test sits on the white-noise diagonal ($D = 0.019$, $p = 0.856$), confirming the model removes all linear spectral structure. The only remaining structure is the nonlinear ARCH effect identified by McLeod-Li.

12. Conclusion¶

My goal was to model and forecast overnight EEG alpha power with a fully manual, transparent time-series pipeline and to perform spectral analysis. The goals were met, with honest caveats documented throughout.

  • Transformation & stationarity. A log transform stabilised the multiplicative variance (rolling std proportional to rolling mean). ADF strongly rejected a unit root in $\log(X_t)$ (stat $= -7.69$, $p \approx 10^{-11}$), so $d = 0$ was adopted. An initial investigation of $d = 1$ revealed a near-unit MA root (1.006) in the best d=1 candidate — the diagnostic signature of over-differencing — confirming that differencing was unnecessary.

  • Seasonality, re-examined. The physiologically obvious $S = 180$ seasonal model is wrong for this series: seasonal differencing over-differences (variance increases), there is no seasonal ACF/PACF signature at lags 18 or 180, and the ultradian rhythm is spectrally broad across 66–95 minutes rather than locked to a single period. A non-seasonal model was used and the rhythm documented spectrally.

  • Final model. $\text{ARMA}(2,0,1)$ on $\log(X_t)$, chosen by AICc (698.33) over four d=0 alternatives and confirmed by root diagnostics (AR roots 4.81 and 1.053; MA root 1.327 — all well-separated): $$(1 - 1.157\,B + 0.197\,B^2)(Y_t + 25.75) = (1 - 0.753\,B)\,\varepsilon_t, \qquad \hat\sigma^2 = 0.081$$ Residuals pass Ljung-Box ($p = 0.71$) and the spectral white-noise tests (Fisher $p = 0.198$, KS $p = 0.856$); they fail McLeod-Li ($p \sim 10^{-8}$), revealing conditional heteroscedasticity — the clearest direction for future work via an ARMA+GARCH extension.

  • Forecasting. 1-step-ahead MAPE of 19.6%, beating persistence (23.2%) and naive-seasonal (31.2%) baselines. Prediction intervals are constant-width, a known limitation given the ARCH structure.

  • Spectral analysis. The periodogram of the linearly detrended $\log(X_t)$ localises the ultradian band at 66–95 minutes (Fisher $p \approx 5.6 \times 10^{-46}$). The theoretical spectral density of the fitted ARMA(2,0,1) shows a smooth $1/f$-type decay consistent with the near-unit AR root, confirming the model captures short-range dependence without encoding the ultradian rhythm as a line frequency. Residual Fisher and KS tests confirm the model leaves white noise behind.

In [14]:
print('=== RESULTS SUMMARY ===')
print(f'Series: {len(X_t)} epochs (~{len(X_t)*EPOCH_SEC/3600:.1f} h)   Train: {len(train)}  Test: {len(test)}')
print(f'Best model: {best["label"]}   AICc={best["aicc"]:.2f}')
print(f'ADF log(X): stat={adf_log["stat"]:.2f} p={adf_log["pval"]:.2g}  -> d=0 adopted')
print(f'1-step MAPE: {np.mean(np.abs((actual-pred_1step)/actual))*100:.2f}%')
print(f'Spectral: series Fisher p={gp_ser:.2g}  |  residual Fisher p={gp_res:.2g}  residual KS p={dp_res:.2g}')

=== RESULTS SUMMARY ===
Series: 2650 epochs (~22.1 h)   Train: 2120  Test: 530
Best model: ARMA(2,0,1)   AICc=698.33
ADF log(X): stat=-7.69 p=1.4e-11  -> d=0 adopted
1-step MAPE: 19.64%
Spectral: series Fisher p=5.6e-46  |  residual Fisher p=0.2  residual KS p=0.86

13. Beyond the required analysis¶

The two checks below are not part of the core Box–Jenkins pipeline for this project. They extend it slighly by testing the conclusions from a second angle.

In [15]:
# (a) KPSS — complementary stationarity test. Note the null is FLIPPED relative to ADF:
#     KPSS H0 = stationary, so a LARGE p-value supports stationarity. Reading it next to ADF
#     triangulates the borderline d decision (the AR root sits at 1.053, close to the boundary).
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import kpss
stat, p, lags, crit = kpss(Y_t.dropna(), regression='c', nlags='auto')
print(f'KPSS [log(X_t)], H0=stationary: stat={stat:.4f}  p={p:.4g}  '
      f'-> {"fail to reject H0 (stationary)" if p > 0.05 else "reject H0 (non-stationary)"}')

# (b) MASE — robust accuracy metric. MAPE is unstable here because alpha power approaches zero
#     in deep sleep (tiny denominators). MASE scales error by the in-sample naive-1 MAE; <1 means
#     the model beats the naive in-sample forecast.
def mase(actual, pred, train_series):
    scale = np.mean(np.abs(np.diff(train_series.values)))   # in-sample naive(1) MAE
    return np.mean(np.abs(actual - pred)) / scale

print(f'MASE  ARMA(2,0,1) 1-step : {mase(actual, pred_1step, train):.3f}')
print(f'MASE  persistence       : {mase(actual, X_t.shift(1).iloc[split_idx:].values, train):.3f}')

KPSS [log(X_t)], H0=stationary: stat=1.1455  p=0.01  -> reject H0 (non-stationary)
MASE  ARMA(2,0,1) 1-step : 0.860
MASE  persistence       : 1.022

KPSS (complementary stationarity test). KPSS $H_0$ is stationarity — the opposite null from ADF. The result (stat $= 1.146$, $p = 0.01$) rejects $H_0$, so KPSS calls the series non-stationary while ADF rejected the unit root. This conflict is characteristic of a near-unit-root process: the AR root at 1.053 sits close enough to the boundary that KPSS detects the slow mean reversion as nonstationarity, while ADF, which tests only for an AR unit root, rejects it. Neither test is wrong; the series genuinely occupies the borderline. In this situation the model-based diagnostic from Section 8 is the deciding evidence: fitting $d = 1$ produced an MA root of 1.006, the signature of over-differencing, which rules out $d = 1$ regardless of what the univariate tests suggest. The conflict here is expected, not a failure of the analysis.

MASE (robust forecast accuracy). MAPE is unstable for this series because alpha power approaches zero in deep slow-wave sleep, inflating the percentage error on those epochs. MASE scales the mean absolute error by the in-sample one-step naive MAE, so values below 1 indicate the model beats the naive baseline. The ARMA(2,0,1) 1-step MASE of 0.860 confirms it does; the persistence MASE of 1.022 shows that even persistence does not consistently beat the naive on this scale, making the model's improvement genuine.

Acknowledgements.¶

Data from the PhysioNet Sleep-EDF Expanded Database.

References¶

  1. Kemp B. et al. (2000). A simple format for exchange of digitized polygraphic recordings. Electroenceph. Clin. Neurophysiol. 69(4), 391–395.
  2. Goldberger A.L. et al. (2000). PhysioBank, PhysioToolkit, and PhysioNet. Circulation 101(23), e215–e220.
  3. Box, Jenkins, Reinsel, Ljung (2015). Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control, 5th ed. Wiley.
  4. Hurvich & Tsai (1989). Regression and time series model selection in small samples. Biometrika 76(2), 297–307.
  5. McLeod & Li (1983). Diagnostic checking ARMA time series models using squared-residual autocorrelations. J. Time Ser. Anal. 4(4), 269–273.
